ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for tofu skin salad (Liang Ban Fu Zhu); closest match is the general tofu food product class. Tofu skin (yuba/fu zhu) is a distinct soy product not separately enumerated in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Soya bean products — tofu and tofu-based dishes
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers soya bean products including tofu; no specific code exists for cold-dressed tofu skin salad. Used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for tofu skin salad as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Tofu Skin Salad is approximately 150 g, representing a standard side-dish portion as commonly served in Chinese restaurants and home meals.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Tofu, raw (NDB 16126) and Yuba (tofu skin) reference entries; supplemented by Singapore HPB Food Composition Database for soy-based dish sodium and macro context
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Liang Ban Fu Zhu (cold tofu skin salad) as a composite dish. Macronutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for tofu skin / yuba (high-protein soy product) combined with HPB Singapore soy-based dish references. Sodium reflects the addition of soy sauce dressing typical of this preparation. Values from the input enriched JSON (confidence 0.95) are consistent with these approved-source anchors and have been retained where they align. Saturated fat estimated from USDA soy product fatty acid profiles.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — soy and legume products; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Liang Ban Fu Zhu (cold tofu skin salad) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 25 is estimated by analogy with soy-based products (tofu, yuba) which consistently show GI values in the 15–32 range in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). The high protein (12.5 g/100 g) and moderate fat (8.2 g/100 g) content of tofu skin further attenuate glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (25 × 8.25) / 100 ≈ 2.1 (based on 150 g serving providing 8.25 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce dressing (380 mg/100 g; ~570 mg per 150 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium soy sauce, as a standard 150 g serving contributes approximately 570 mg sodium — roughly 25% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~25) and high plant protein content (12.5 g/100 g) supporting stable postprandial glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tofu Skin Salad is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI (~25), high protein content from tofu skin, and low carbohydrate load (~8.25 g per 150 g serving), all of which support glycemic control; however, sodium from the soy sauce dressing should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Plant-based protein, low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and soy isoflavones with cardioprotective potential
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tofu skin is a cholesterol-free, low-saturated-fat plant protein source containing soy isoflavones that may modestly reduce LDL cholesterol; the primary cardiovascular caution is the sodium contributed by the soy sauce dressing, which should be minimized in individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Soy isoflavones and phytochemicals with potential anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Soy-based foods such as tofu skin contain isoflavones that may have modest anti-inflammatory effects relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; this dish is generally considered suitable as part of an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tofu Skin Salad, known in Mandarin as Liángbàn Fǔzhú (凉拌腐竹) and in Cantonese as Leung Ban Fu Zuk, is a popular Chinese cold appetizer made from rehydrated dried tofu skin (yuba or fu zhu). The preparation involves soaking dried tofu skin ribbons until pliable, briefly blanching them, and then tossing them in a savory dressing of light soy sauce, black vinegar, minced garlic, sesame oil, and optionally chili oil. The dish is commonly garnished with julienned cucumber, carrot, and fresh coriander, adding color, crunch, and freshness. Tofu skin has a distinctively chewy, silky texture and a mild, slightly nutty flavor that absorbs the dressing well. Nutritionally, this dish is notable for its high plant-based protein content (~12.5 g/100 g), moderate fat from sesame oil, low carbohydrate content (~5.5 g/100 g), and a low glycemic index of approximately 25, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or seeking high-protein, low-GI foods. It provides meaningful amounts of calcium (150 mg/100 g), iron (2.8 mg/100 g), and magnesium (65 mg/100 g). The primary clinical caution is its sodium content (~380 mg/100 g) from the soy sauce dressing, which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. This dish is vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free, and is a staple appetizer or side dish in Northern Chinese and Cantonese cuisine, widely consumed across East Asia and in Chinese diaspora communities globally.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Liang Ban Fu Zhu as a composite dish. Macronutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central tofu skin / yuba entries and HPB Singapore soy-based dish references, and are consistent with the input enriched JSON (confidence 0.95). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database soy product data. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown are estimated from USDA soy product profiles. Confidence is therefore rated medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.8 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (4.5 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central fatty acid profiles for tofu skin / yuba (NDB 16126 and related entries); these values are not directly reported in the input JSON and represent adapted estimates. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite cold-dressed dish (rehydrated tofu skin with dressing) is not directly reported in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as a prepared dish; the rehydration step significantly alters moisture relative to dry tofu skin values, making a reliable estimate unavailable from approved sources. USDA FoodData Central entry for raw firm tofu (NDB 16126) and yuba entries were consulted but do not provide a directly applicable moisture value for the rehydrated and dressed preparation. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero — tofu skin is a plant-derived product containing no vitamin B12 by nature of its plant origin, consistent with USDA FoodData Central soy product entries. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero — tofu skin is a plant-derived product with no significant vitamin D content unless fortified; confirmed per USDA FoodData Central soy product entries. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — tofu skin is a plant-derived product containing no cholesterol, consistent with HPB and USDA data. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible / naturally zero for minimally processed soy-based products per USDA FoodData Central. 7. The duplicate portion entry in the input JSON (two identical '1 serving (150g)' entries) has been deduplicated; a single 150 g serving is used. 8. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Liang Ban Fu Zhu was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 9. Sodium content (380 mg/100 g) reflects a typical soy-sauce-dressed preparation; actual sodium will vary significantly with the amount of soy sauce used.
